title: Writing Queries
intro: Your task manager's database is set up and the tables are ready. Now you need to write the SQL queries that power every feature. Think of queries like asking a librarian for exactly the right book — be precise and you get what you need, be vague and you get the wrong shelf. One wrong query and users could see each other's tasks — or lose data entirely.

teach.title: CRUD: The Four Operations
teach.body: Every database interaction falls into one of four categories: **Create** (INSERT), **Read** (SELECT), **Update** (UPDATE), and **Delete** (DELETE). Your task manager needs all four: creating new tasks, fetching the task list, marking tasks complete, and deleting tasks. Each operation is a SQL query that tells the database exactly what to do.

teach.title: The Queries Behind Your Task Manager
teach.annotation: Notice: every UPDATE and DELETE includes AND user_id = '...'. This prevents users from modifying other people's tasks. Never forget this filter.

teach.title: Queries Are Like Asking a Librarian
teach.body: You don't wander through every shelf in a library to find a book. You ask the librarian: 'I need all books by this author, sorted by year.' SQL queries work the same way — you tell the database exactly what you want (SELECT), where to look (FROM/WHERE), and how to sort it (ORDER BY). A vague request gets you the wrong books; a precise query gets exactly what you need.

CRUD queries are the engine behind every feature in your app. Master them, and you can build any feature users ask for — task lists, filters, search, dashboards. Each new feature is a reason for users to stay and pay.
Always filter queries by user_id to enforce data ownership. A query without a user filter is a security hole — it could let anyone read, modify, or delete another user's tasks.

start.narrative: Your task manager needs a 'show my tasks' feature. You're writing the query to fetch a user's tasks from the database. How do you write it?

choices.text: SELECT * FROM tasks; — grab everything, filter in the frontend
SELECT * FROM tasks WHERE user_id = $1 ORDER BY created_at DESC;
SELECT * FROM tasks WHERE user_id = $1;

no-filter.narrative: Your query returns every task from every user in the database. During testing with 3 users, User A can see User B's private tasks. A beta tester reports the bug publicly. How do you respond?

choices.text: Immediately add a WHERE user_id = $1 clause and deploy a hotfix
Filter the results in JavaScript on the frontend before rendering

good-query.narrative: Your query works perfectly — each user only sees their own tasks, sorted newest first. Time to add interactions.

choices.text: Move on to the UPDATE query for marking tasks complete

missing-order.narrative: The query returns the right tasks, but they appear in a random order. Users complain that newly created tasks show up at the bottom or in the middle of the list. How do you fix it?

choices.text: Add ORDER BY created_at DESC to show newest tasks first
Sort the tasks in JavaScript after fetching them

update-query.narrative: The list works. Now users need to mark tasks complete. How do you write the UPDATE query?

choices.text: UPDATE tasks SET is_complete = true WHERE id = $1 AND user_id = $2;
UPDATE tasks SET is_complete = true WHERE id = $1;
UPDATE tasks SET is_complete = true; — fix the WHERE later

delete-query.narrative: Tasks pile up. Users want to delete the ones they no longer need. How do you write the DELETE query?

choices.text: DELETE FROM tasks WHERE id = $1 AND user_id = $2;
Add a deleted_at timestamp instead and filter it out of every SELECT, so undelete stays possible
DELETE FROM tasks WHERE id = $1; — anyone with the ID can wipe a row

performance.narrative: Power users now have thousands of tasks each. The list query takes 2+ seconds and the page feels slow. How do you fix performance without changing the query shape?

choices.text: Add an index on (user_id, created_at) so the database can find and sort matching rows directly
Add LIMIT and OFFSET to paginate the results — fetch 50 tasks at a time instead of all of them
Leave the query — 2 seconds is fine, users can wait

rls.narrative: Your queries are correct, but you noticed in the news that another startup leaked data because a single forgotten WHERE clause exposed everyone's records. You want a safety net so a future bug can't do the same. What do you set up?

choices.text: Enable Row Level Security on the tasks table and write a policy: USING (auth.uid() = user_id). The database enforces the user filter even if the application code forgets it.
Add a checklist item to every PR: 'did you include user_id in WHERE?' and trust reviewers to catch mistakes
Skip RLS — your queries are correct, no extra layer needed

excellent-end.narrative: Your queries are filtered, sorted, indexed, and protected by Row Level Security. Even if a future contributor writes a query without WHERE user_id, the database silently scopes the result to the logged-in user. Defense in depth.
excellent-end.feedback: This is production-grade query work. You filtered for ownership, ordered for predictability, indexed for performance, and added RLS as a safety net. The combination means a single careless query cannot leak data — the database itself refuses.

mediocre-end.narrative: Your queries work today. Six months later, a junior contributor adds a new endpoint and forgets the user_id filter. Code review misses it. Production silently leaks data until a user reports it.
mediocre-end.feedback: Code review is necessary but not sufficient. Humans miss things — that's why we add automated safeguards. Row Level Security shifts the responsibility from 'every developer remembers' to 'the database enforces.'

weak-end.narrative: Without RLS, the tasks table trusts whatever query the application sends. One typo, one bug, one rushed pull request and the entire dataset is exposed. You're one mistake away from a serious incident.
weak-end.feedback: Skipping RLS on a multi-tenant table is a ticking time bomb. Even careful teams ship buggy queries eventually. RLS makes the database your last line of defense — turn it on for every table that holds user data.
